Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I tried to set up a schedule at the beginning of the semester, but with limited resources at home and needing to help my family out, things rarely went according to plan. I ended up studying whenever I could grab time—late at night mostly, or early mornings before anyone else woke up. I relied heavily on listening to recorded lectures because I learn better that way, so I'd play them while doing other things around the house.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course materials, and then when deadlines approached, I'd cram in as many hours as possible. I wish I could say I was more consistent, but life just kept throwing things at me. The anxiety of falling behind actually made it harder to start studying sometimes, which sounds backwards but that's how it felt.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or seeing a message from one of my peer study partners that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a reminder email and feel that familiar knot in my stomach, knowing I was behind. The peer support group we had was actually really helpful—when someone posted that they were working on something, it motivated me to jump on too. Fear was honestly a big motivator, as much as I hate admitting that.
Q4: There were a few weeks in the middle of the semester where logging in started to feel more automatic. I think it happened because I connected with a few classmates through the discussion forums and we started checking in with each other regularly. Having that social accountability made the LMS feel less like a chore and more like meeting up with people. But when things got stressful at home, that routine fell apart pretty quickly.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I'd often play them multiple times, sometimes at 1.5x speed when I was short on time. The discussion forums were also really valuable because hearing other students explain concepts in different ways helped things click for me. When peers broke down difficult topics in their own words, it was sometimes clearer than the official materials.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials, to be honest. Long PDFs and textbook chapters just didn't work for my learning style, and with limited internet, downloading them was a hassle. I also skipped a lot of the optional quizzes because when you're already stressed and anxious about the required work, optional stuff feels impossible to prioritize. I know I probably missed out on practice opportunities, but I had to make choices about where to put my energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to assignments or exams. If I finished something and felt like I actually understood the material better, that was rewarding. Also, activities where I could interact with other students felt more meaningful because studying alone all the time gets isolating. The social element made it feel like we were all struggling together, which sounds weird but was comforting.
Q8: Required activities came with this weight of anxiety—like I had to do them or face consequences. Optional ones felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was already scrambling to complete the mandatory stuff. Sometimes I'd look at optional activities and think 'that would actually be helpful,' but then the fear of not finishing required work would win out. It's frustrating because I know the optional stuff could have helped me learn better.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them offline constantly because my internet isn't always reliable. I also took handwritten notes while listening and reviewed those instead of going back to the LMS. Sometimes I'd record myself summarizing concepts and play those back—it sounds silly but hearing my own voice explain things helped me remember. A lot of my real studying happened completely disconnected from the platform.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes for hours, or listening to downloaded lectures while lying in bed trying to understand difficult concepts. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I also had study sessions with a neighbor who was taking similar courses—we'd quiz each other verbally, which is perfect for how I learn. The LMS only saw me when I was online, but learning happened everywhere.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then that material would live on my phone or in my notebook for weeks. I'd return to the same concepts repeatedly without ever logging back in to access them again. My phone became like my portable classroom. I think my actual engagement with the course content was way higher than my login frequency would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely periods where everything felt impossible. When my anxiety spiked—usually around midterms or when multiple assignments piled up—I'd almost freeze. I'd open the LMS, see everything I was behind on, and just close it because the fear was overwhelming. Family obligations also pulled me away; when you have limited resources, you can't always prioritize school over helping out at home.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from working or helping my family, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid starting things because I was scared of doing them wrong. The competing responsibilities were constant—I had to choose between studying and other obligations almost daily. It affected not just how much I used the LMS, but how effectively I used it.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd watch a video but realize at the end that I hadn't actually processed any of it because my mind was elsewhere. I'd be thinking about bills, or family stuff, or just feeling too anxious to focus. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system—video watched, time logged—but mentally I was completely checked out. Those sessions felt like wasted time, which only added to my stress.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made it hard to start because I was scared of failing or not understanding the material. It's like the more important something was, the more terrifying it felt to attempt it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more prepared, but that tomorrow kept getting pushed back. It's a cycle I'm still trying to break.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd be relatively inactive for days, then suddenly log in frantically when something was due soon. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of a deadline was sometimes the only thing that could push through my anxiety and get me to actually submit work. I'd watch the clock counting down and feel this mix of terror and focus that somehow got things done.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it gave me a boost of motivation to engage more. Knowing that my effort was recognized made the struggle feel worthwhile. But negative feedback or poor grades sometimes made me want to avoid the course entirely for a few days. The peer support helped balance this out—my study partners would remind me that one bad grade wasn't the end of everything.
Q18: The course structure was honestly a bit overwhelming with 11 online courses running simultaneously. Having so many things to track made it hard to stay organized, especially with my limited resources. The modules that had clear, step-by-step progressions were easier to follow. But when things were scattered or the expectations weren't clear, my anxiety would spike and I'd disengage rather than risk doing something wrong.
Q19: The availability of video content made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. The discussion forums were also designed well for peer interaction, which became my lifeline. What made it harder was when assignments had vague instructions or when there were too many small tasks to track. Also, some resources required consistent internet access, which was a barrier for me given my situation at home.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying really hard to understand the material but struggling to produce work they feel confident about. They might watch videos repeatedly, read discussions, search for help—all showing as activity—but then feel too uncertain or scared to actually submit assignments. That fear of failure can be paralyzing. Or maybe they're dealing with personal stuff that eats up the energy needed to complete work.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I didn't have super high LMS activity, but I still managed to pass because a lot of my real learning happened offline. Listening to downloaded lectures, studying from notes, working with peers in person—none of that showed up in the system. Someone might look disengaged online but be working really hard in ways the platform can't see. The LMS only captures one dimension of learning.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns—like when someone logs in, how long they spend on videos, whether they're meeting deadlines. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture anxiety levels, family responsibilities, or whether someone was actually focused during their logged time. They see the surface but not the depth of what's really going on with a student.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, connecting them to things I already know, discussing them with peers. It's showing up even when I'm scared, asking for help when I'm confused, and not giving up when things get hard. Engagement is as much about mental investment as it is about completing tasks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support to keep going, or how many times I almost gave up but pushed through anyway. It wouldn't capture the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the pride I felt when I finally understood something difficult. It can't see that I'm a first-generation student with limited resources who's fighting to be here. My resilience and determination don't leave data trails, but they're the main reasons I passed.
